children.name: Columba
children.description: The core genus of 'true pigeons' — medium to large, stout-bodied birds with small rounded heads and a rapid, direct flight. The Rock Dove is the ancestor of all domestic and feral pigeons worldwide. Several island-endemic species in the Macaronesian archipelagos (Canaries, Madeira) survive in relict laurel forests that once covered much of the Mediterranean.

children.name: Columba livia
children.description: Columba livia (Rock Dove) is a pigeons species found in Europe and Asia and Africa regions.

children.name: Columba oenas
children.description: Columba oenas (Stock Dove) is a pigeons species found in Europe and Asia regions.

children.name: Columba palumbus
children.description: Columba palumbus (Common Wood Pigeon) is a pigeons species found in Europe and Asia and Africa regions.

children.name: Columba eversmanni
children.description: Columba eversmanni (Yellow-eyed Pigeon) is a pigeons species found in Asia region.

children.name: Columba rupestris
children.description: Columba rupestris (Hill Pigeon) is a pigeons species found in Asia region.

children.name: Columba leuconota
children.description: Columba leuconota (Snow Pigeon) is a pigeons species found in Asia region.

children.name: Columba trocaz
children.description: Columba trocaz (Trocaz Pigeon) is a pigeons species found in Europe region.

children.name: Columba bollii
children.description: Columba bollii (Bolle's Pigeon) is a pigeons species found in Africa region.

children.name: Columba junoniae
children.description: Columba junoniae (Laurel Pigeon) is a pigeons species found in Africa region.

children.name: Columba albinucha
children.description: A dark grey pigeon of Central African rainforests; white patch on the nape is diagnostic.

children.name: Columba arquatrix
children.description: Large yellow-eyed pigeon of African montane forests; feeds on fruit in the upper canopy.

children.name: Columba guinea
children.description: A widespread African pigeon with rusty-red speckled wings; common in towns and farmlands.

children.name: Columba delegorguei
children.description: A small forest pigeon from eastern and southern Africa; named after the French naturalist.

children.name: Columba palumboides
children.description: A large dark pigeon endemic to the Andaman and Nicobar Islands.

children.name: Columba elphinstonii
children.description: A vulnerable species from the Western Ghats of India; distinctive maroon mantle.

children.name: Columba torringtoniae
children.description: Endemic to Sri Lanka's montane forests; purplish-brown upperparts.

children.name: Columba punicea
children.description: A rare pigeon of Southeast Asian lowland forests; distinctive silvery crown.

children.name: Columba arquatrices
children.description: A recently split species from the rock dove complex; restricted to southern Africa.

children.name: Columba vitiensis
children.description: A widespread pigeon of Australasian islands with iridescent green-purple plumage; also called White-throated Pigeon.

children.name: Columba hodgsonii
children.description: A Himalayan species finely speckled with white on the upperparts; named after Brian Hodgson.

children.name: Columba pulchricollis
children.description: A dark grey pigeon of Himalayan and Southeast Asian forests ashy-grey neck and pale head.

children.name: Streptopelia
children.description: The turtle doves and collared doves — generally slimmer and longer-tailed than Columba, with a gentle, repetitive cooing. The Eurasian Collared Dove staged one of the most remarkable range expansions of the 20th century, spreading from Turkey across all of Europe within decades. The European Turtle Dove, by contrast, has suffered steep declines driven by agricultural change and hunting pressure on migration.

children.name: Streptopelia decaocto
children.description: Streptopelia decaocto (Eurasian Collared Dove) is a doves species found in Europe and Asia regions.

children.name: Streptopelia turtur
children.description: Streptopelia turtur (European Turtle Dove) is a doves species found in Europe and Asia and Africa regions.

children.name: Streptopelia orientalis
children.description: Streptopelia orientalis (Oriental Turtle Dove) is a doves species found in Asia region.

children.name: Streptopelia tranquebarica
children.description: Streptopelia tranquebarica (Red Turtle Dove) is a doves species found in Asia region.

children.name: Streptopelia chinensis
children.description: Streptopelia chinensis (Spotted Dove) is a doves species found in Asia region.

children.name: Streptopelia senegalensis
children.description: Streptopelia senegalensis (Laughing Dove) is a doves species found in Africa and Asia regions.

children.name: Streptopelia capicola
children.description: A common African dove with a distinctive black collar at the back of the neck.

children.name: Streptopelia vinacea
children.description: A pale vinous-pink dove of the Sahel and Sudanian savanna; named for its wine-coloured breast.

children.name: Streptopelia lugens
children.description: Found in the Horn of Africa and southwestern Arabia; prefers juniper woodlands.

children.name: Streptopelia bitorquata
children.description: A dove of Indonesian islands; two black collar bands on the nape.

children.name: Streptopelia hypopyrrha
children.description: A West African dove restricted to the Adamawa Plateau.

children.name: Streptopelia roseogrisea
children.description: A pale dove of the Sahel and Arabian region; the wild ancestor of the domesticated Ringneck Dove.

children.name: Streptopelia decipiens
children.description: A widespread African dove of savanna and woodland; grey forehead and black half-collar.

children.name: Streptopelia semitorquata
children.description: The largest African Streptopelia; red eyes and dark grey head with a black collar.

children.name: Treron
children.description: Green pigeons — compact, frugivorous pigeons with predominantly green plumage that camouflages them in forest canopies. They are highly specialised fruit-eaters with muscular gizzards that can grind hard seeds, and they play an important role as seed dispersers in Asian tropical and subtropical forests.

children.name: Treron sieboldii
children.description: Treron sieboldii (White-bellied Green Pigeon) is a green pigeons species found in Asia region.

children.name: Treron formosae
children.description: Treron formosae (Whistling Green Pigeon) is a green pigeons species found in Asia region.

children.name: Treron vernalis
children.description: A colourful green pigeon of Southeast Asia; males have a pink neck and orange chest.

children.name: Treron curvirostra
children.description: Named for its unusually thick curved bill; feeds on hard drupes and figs.

children.name: Treron olax
children.description: The smallest Treron in Southeast Asia; olive-green with a yellow vent.

children.name: Treron bicinctus
children.description: A green pigeon with a bright orange patch on the breast and two yellow wing bars.

children.name: Treron phoenicopterus
children.description: A large green pigeon of South Asia; bright yellow feet and a grey crown.

children.name: Treron pompadora
children.description: A widespread green pigeon complex ranging from India to the Philippines; maroon wing coverts.

children.name: Chalcophaps
children.description: Emerald doves — small, ground-dwelling doves with brilliant iridescent green wings contrasting with vinous underparts. They walk quietly through forest floors foraging for fallen seeds and fruit, flushing with explosive wing-claps when disturbed. Their jewel-like plumage is unique among Palearctic columbids.

children.name: Chalcophaps indica
children.description: Chalcophaps indica (Common Emerald Dove) is a doves species found in Asia region.

children.name: Chalcophaps stephani
children.description: A small emerald dove of Sulawesi, New Guinea and the Bismarck Archipelago; named after the naturalist.

children.name: Oena
children.description: A monotypic genus containing the Namaqua Dove — a small, long-tailed dove of arid open habitats across Africa and the Arabian Peninsula. Males have a distinctive black face and breast, and the species is one of the smallest columbids. It inhabits dry savannas, scrublands, and semi-desert edges.

children.name: Oena capensis
children.description: Oena capensis (Namaqua Dove) is a doves species found in Africa and Asia regions.

children.name: Raphus
children.description: The dodo — a giant flightless pigeon endemic to Mauritius, driven extinct in the 17th century within decades of human arrival. The dodo is the world's most famous symbol of human-caused extinction.

children.name: Raphus cucullatus
children.description: A large flightless pigeon endemic to Mauritius, extinct by the late 1600s after sailors and introduced animals destroyed it. Standing about a meter tall, it has become the archetypal emblem of extinction ('dead as a dodo').

children.name: Ectopistes
children.description: The passenger pigeon — once the most abundant bird in North America, numbering in the billions, hunted to extinction by 1914. A landmark case in conservation history.

children.name: Ectopistes migratorius
children.description: Formerly the most numerous bird in North America, with flocks of billions darkening the sky for days. Relentless commercial hunting and habitat loss drove it extinct; the last individual, Martha, died in 1914.

children.name: Patagioenas
children.description: New World pigeons of the Americas, formerly placed in Columba. They range from the band-tailed pigeon of North American forests to colorful Neotropical species.

children.name: Patagioenas fasciata
children.description: A large forest pigeon of western North America and the Andes, named for the pale band across its tail. The closest living relative of the extinct passenger pigeon.

children.name: Patagioenas leucocephala
children.description: A pigeon of Caribbean and south Florida mangroves with a gleaming white crown. Near Threatened, dependent on coastal forests and fruiting trees for food.

children.name: Patagioenas plumbea
children.description: A dark grey Amazonian pigeon with a purplish iridescence on the neck.

children.name: Patagioenas cayennensis
children.description: Widespread in Central and South America; distinctive pale vent and white throat patch.

children.name: Patagioenas subnacea
children.description: A medium-sized pigeon of lowland forests; rich rufous-brown plumage with red eyes.

children.name: Patagioenas subnigra
children.description: A dark pigeon of the Andes; inhabits cloud forest at 1500-3000 m.

children.name: Patagioenas speziosa
children.description: A large Amazonian pigeon with scaled neck pattern.

children.name: Patagioenas aramea
children.description: A widespread Caribbean and Central American pigeon; red bill with white tip.

children.name: Patagioenas flavirostris
children.description: A common pigeon of Mexico and Central America with a bright red bill marked with white.

children.name: Patagioenas picazuro
children.description: A large pigeon of Brazil and neighbouring countries; white wing crescents visible in flight.

children.name: Patagioenas maculosa
children.description: A South American pigeon of open woodlands; white spots on the wings in flight.

children.name: Patagioenas araucana
children.description: A large pigeon of Chilean and Argentine temperate forests; named after the Araucaria tree.

children.name: Goura
children.description: Crowned pigeons of New Guinea — the largest living pigeons, turkey-sized birds with spectacular lacy crests. All are threatened by hunting and habitat loss.

children.name: Goura victoria
children.description: The largest living pigeon, a blue-grey New Guinea bird with a magnificent white-tipped lacy crest. Named after Queen Victoria; Near Threatened due to hunting for meat and plumes.

children.name: Goura cristata
children.description: A turkey-sized blue-grey ground pigeon of western New Guinea with a fan-like crest. Vulnerable, declining from hunting and forest loss.

children.name: Goura scheepmakeri
children.description: A large crowned pigeon of southeastern New Guinea; deep maroon breast and grey-tipped crest. Vulnerable.

children.name: Ducula
children.description: Imperial pigeons — large, canopy-dwelling fruit pigeons of Asia, Australasia and Pacific islands, important seed dispersers of tropical forests.

children.name: Ducula aenea
children.description: A large fruit-eating pigeon of South and Southeast Asian forests with metallic green wings and a pale grey head. A key disperser of large rainforest seeds.

children.name: Ducula myristicivora
children.description: Endemic to the Moluccas; feeds on nutmeg and other fruits; vital seed disperser.

children.name: Ducula rufigaster
children.description: A colourful imperial pigeon from New Guinea; rufous belly and metallic green upperparts.

children.name: Ducula zoeae
children.description: A beautiful species from New Guinea; grey head, purple breast, and chestnut belly.

children.name: Ducula rubricera
children.description: A large pigeon of the Bismarck Archipelago and Solomon Islands; red bill knob.

children.name: Ducula concinna
children.description: A beautifully coloured imperial pigeon with a bronze-green sheen.

children.name: Ducula forsteni
children.description: A Sulawesi endemic; named after the Dutch naturalist Forsten.

children.name: Ducula bicolor
children.description: A striking black-and-white pigeon of Southeast Asian and Australasian coasts and islands, pure white with black flight feathers.

children.name: Ducula spilorrhoa
children.description: A white imperial pigeon of northern Australia and New Guinea; black wing tips and yellow bill.

children.name: Ducula badia
children.description: A large pigeon of Asian montane forests; maroon underparts and a distinctive low resonant call.

children.name: Ducula mullerii
children.description: A colourful imperial pigeon of lowland New Guinea; pinkish neck and collar.

children.name: Ptilinopus
children.description: Fruit doves — small, brilliantly colored doves of the Indo-Pacific and Australasia, among the most vividly plumaged of all pigeons.

children.name: Ptilinopus magnificus
children.description: A large, gaudily colored fruit dove of Australian and New Guinean rainforests, with green, purple and yellow plumage. Named for its deep 'wollack-wa-hoo' call.

children.name: Ptilinopus pulchellus
children.description: A small, jewel-like New Guinean fruit dove with a crimson cap and multicolored underparts. Feeds in the rainforest canopy on small fruits.

children.name: Ptilinopus marshallianus
children.description: A rare fruit dove from New Guinea; named after the collector.

children.name: Ptilinopus ornatus
children.description: An ornate green and yellow fruit dove from the mountains of New Guinea.

children.name: Ptilinopus pearlii
children.description: A recently described fruit dove from Fiji; distinctive orange cap and green body.

children.name: Ptilinopus melanospilus
children.description: A green fruit dove with a yellow throat and black nape patch; widespread from Java to the Philippines.

children.name: Ptilinopus fischeri
children.description: A montane fruit dove of Sulawesi and the Moluccas; deep red ear patch and yellow bill.

children.name: Ptilinopus regina
children.description: A beautifully coloured fruit dove of Australian and Indonesian rainforests; rose-pink crown.

children.name: Ptilinopus superbus
children.description: Among the most colourful of all pigeons, with a purple crown, green cheeks, orange nape and red breast band.

children.name: Ptilinopus perousii
children.description: A striking fruit dove of Fiji, Tonga and Samoa; male has a crimson crown, white face and yellow belly.

children.name: Zenaida
children.description: American mourning and zenaida doves, including one of the most abundant and widely hunted gamebirds of North America.

children.name: Zenaida macroura
children.description: One of the most abundant and widespread birds in North America, named for its plaintive cooing. A major game species, with hundreds of millions harvested annually yet remaining numerous.

children.name: Zenaida asiatica
children.description: Common in the southern US and Mexico; expanding northward; named for bold white wing patches.

children.name: Zenaida gracili
children.description: One of the most abundant South American doves; named for the dark ear patch.

children.name: Zenaida aurita
children.description: A Caribbean dove named after Zenaide, wife of the naturalist Charles Lucien Bonaparte.

children.name: Turtur
children.description: Small terrestrial doves of sub-Saharan Africa that produce a distinctive whistling wing sound in flight.

children.name: Turtur chalcospilos
children.description: Named for metallic green spots on wings; produces low resonant cooing from forest floor.

children.name: Turtur abyssinicus
children.description: A Sahelian species; black bill distinguishes it from the similar emerald-spotted dove.

children.name: Turtur afer
children.description: Widespread in West and Central African forests; iridescent blue wing spots.

children.name: Turtur tympanistria
children.description: A small dove of African forests; white face and breast, black and green back; named for its drum-like wing sound.

children.name: Caloenas
children.description: Large, iridescent ground pigeons of Southeast Asian islands; the only living relative of the dodo.

children.name: Caloenas nicobarica
children.description: The closest living relative of the extinct dodo; iridescent bronze and green plumage; forages on forest floors of small islands.

children.name: Macropygia
children.description: Slender long-tailed doves of Southeast Asian and Australasian forests.

children.name: Macropygia magna
children.description: A large cuckoo-dove from Sulawesi and neighbouring islands.

children.name: Macropygia nigrirostris
children.description: A barred species from New Guinea and the Bismarck Archipelago.

children.name: Macropygia emberoides
children.description: A rufous-coloured species from the Philippines; long graduated tail.

children.name: Leptotila
children.description: Medium-sized, ground-foraging doves of the Neotropics, often with white-tipped tails and subtle pastel colouration. They forage quietly on forest floors, walking with a nodding head motion.

children.name: Leptotila verreauxi
children.description: The most widespread Leptotila, ranging from Texas to Argentina; pale buff underparts and white tail tips.

children.name: Leptotila rufaxilla
children.description: A forest dove of Amazonia and the Guianas; distinguished by a grey forehead and white throat.

children.name: Leptotila pallida
children.description: A pale dove of the Chocó region of Colombia and Ecuador; poorly known.

children.name: Leptotila plumbeiceps
children.description: A grey-headed dove of Central American and northern South American lowland forests.

children.name: Leptotila ochraceiventris
children.description: An Ecuadorian and Peruvian dove with ochre-yellow underparts; Near Threatened due to habitat loss.

children.name: Geotrygon
children.description: Stout, terrestrial doves of Neotropical forests, named for their quail-like habits. Most are brilliantly coloured with iridescent patches on the head and neck.

children.name: Geotrygon montana
children.description: The most widespread Geotrygon, found from Mexico to Argentina; rich rufous upperparts.

children.name: Geotrygon violacea
children.description: A beautiful quail-dove with violet-purple sheen on the nape and breast.

children.name: Geotrygon versicolor
children.description: Endemic to Jamaica; a large quail-dove with a prominent crest and multicoloured iridescence.

children.name: Geotrygon leucometopia
children.description: A Hispaniolan endemic; white forehead and throat contrast with dark body. Endangered.

children.name: Zentrygon
children.description: Terrestrial doves of Central and South American forests, formerly placed in Geotrygon. They are secretive and walk on the forest floor, flushing only at close range.

children.name: Zentrygon linearis
children.description: A montane quail-dove of the Andes; black-and-white facial stripes and olive-brown body.

children.name: Zentrygon carrikeri
children.description: Endemic to the Sierra de los Tuxtlas in Mexico; endangered due to extreme habitat restriction.

children.name: Claravis
children.description: Small, long-tailed ground doves of the Neotropics. Males are grey or blue-grey with prominent wing spots; females are browner.

children.name: Claravis pretiosa
children.description: A beautiful pale blue dove of lowland forests from Mexico to Argentina.

children.name: Claravis geoffroyi
children.description: A critically endangered dove of southeastern Brazil; purple wing patches. Possibly extinct in the wild.

children.name: Claravis mondetoura
children.description: A highland dove of Central and South American cloud forests; maroon breast band in males.

children.name: Phaps
children.description: Medium-sized, heavily built pigeons of Australia, named for the iridescent bronze or green patches on the wings. They forage on the ground for seeds.

children.name: Phaps chalcoptera
children.description: A common Australian pigeon with iridescent bronze and green wing patches; pinkish breast and pale forehead.

children.name: Phaps elegans
children.description: A smaller bronzewing of dense coastal and mountain forest in southern Australia; dark chestnut back.

children.name: Phaps histrionica
children.description: A nomadic pigeon of arid Australian grasslands; once formed enormous flocks; black and white face pattern.

children.name: Ocyphaps
children.description: A monotypic Australian genus with a distinctive thin black crest and iridescent wing patches. The wings produce a characteristic whistling sound in flight.

children.name: Ocyphaps lophotes
children.description: An Australian pigeon with a distinctive slender black crest; iridescent bronze wing panels and a whistling flight sound.

children.name: Geopelia
children.description: Small, slender doves of Southeast Asia and Australasia with barred plumage patterns. They are terrestrial and typically found in open habitats.

children.name: Geopelia striata
children.description: A small dove of Southeast Asia with fine black-and-white barring; a sweet, gentle song often heard in gardens.

children.name: Geopelia placida
children.description: A small grey-brown dove of Australia and New Guinea with fine barring and a plaintive cooing song.

children.name: Geopelia maugeus
children.description: A small dove of the Lesser Sundas and Moluccas; heavily barred upperparts and pinkish breast.

children.name: Geopelia cuneata
children.description: Australia's smallest dove; grey with white spots on the wings and red eye-rings. A popular aviary bird.

children.name: Chrysoena
children.description: Small fruit doves of Fiji and nearby islands with brilliant golden or orange plumage. Some taxonomies merge them into Ptilinopus.

children.name: Chrysoena victor
children.description: A brilliant orange fruit dove endemic to Fiji; the male is one of the most vividly coloured of all birds.

children.name: Chrysoena luteovirens
children.description: A golden-yellow fruit dove of Fijian forests; the male is entirely bright yellow with greenish tones.
